experiments.hypothesis: The _evict_stale method is called on every _get_messages call, iterating over all conversations each time. Extract the stale user IDs into a list comprehension is fine, but the three separate .pop() calls for each stale user can be consolidated into a helper method that also handles reset(), reducing code duplication between _evict_stale() and reset().
The _send_response method has duplicated logic for sending a message with HTML formatting and plain-text fallback. Extracting this into a small helper method improves DRY and readability.
The file and directory collection logic in backup_workspace uses repetitive patterns that can be simplified with list comprehensions, improving readability and reducing boilerplate.

The cmd_backup function has repetitive code for collecting directories to back up — each directory follows the same pattern of resolving a path from config with a fallback, checking existence, and appending. Extracting this into a loop over a list of (config_key, default_name) tuples improves DRY and readability.

The chunk_messages_by_max_tokens and split_messages_by_token_share functions share nearly identical chunking logic: iterate over messages, accumulate tokens, flush when a threshold is exceeded. Extracting a common helper reduces duplication and improves maintainability.

The parsing of providers and agents uses repetitive dict.get() patterns that mirror the dataclass field defaults. Using a helper function that maps a dict to a dataclass by matching field names and defaults reduces boilerplate and improves DRY/maintainability.

The recover_from_compaction method has three nearly identical blocks that read a file, check if it has content, and append a formatted section. Extracting this into a small helper loop over (path, heading) tuples eliminates the repetition and improves DRY/readability.

The _should_skip_url function iterates over all extensions in _SKIP_EXTENSIONS with a linear loop. Since _SKIP_EXTENSIONS is a frozenset, we can extract the extension from the path using os.path.splitext (or simple string slicing) and do a single O(1) set lookup instead of O(n) iteration. This improves both performance and readability.
